section.title: The Plague of Hail
section.sectionRationale: The seventh plague forms a full, weighty scene: God's declared purpose in raising up Pharaoh, the warning and the chance to shelter, the devastating hail itself, Pharaoh's fullest confession yet, and his hardened heart once the storm stops.
section.thesis: God explains His purpose plainly, that My name might be proclaimed in all the earth, then sends the worst hail Egypt had ever known, and even Pharaoh's confession, the LORD is righteous, and I and my people are wicked, dissolves the instant the thunder stops.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: God's declared purpose and the warning to shelter livestock; the devastating hail on man, beast, and field; Pharaoh's confession and his hardened heart once the storm ends.

units.label: That My name might be proclaimed

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Get up early in the morning, stand before Pharaoh, and tell him that this is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: ‘Let My people go, so that they may worship Me.
Otherwise, I will send all My plagues against you and your officials and your people, so you may know that there is no one like Me in all the earth.
For by this time I could have stretched out My hand and struck you and your people with a plague to wipe you off the earth.
But I have raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display My power to you, and that My {{shem|name}} might be proclaimed in all the earth.
Still, you lord it over My people and do not allow them to go.
Behold, at this time tomorrow I will rain down the worst {{barad|hail}} that has ever fallen on Egypt, from the day it was founded until now.

So give orders now to shelter your livestock and everything you have in the field. Every man or beast that remains in the field and is not brought inside will die when the hail comes down upon them.’”
Those among Pharaoh's officials who feared the word of the LORD hurried to bring their servants and livestock to shelter,
but those who disregarded the word of the LORD left their servants and livestock in the field.

units.label: Fire flashing in the hail
units.note: the worst storm in Egypt's history

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand toward heaven, so that hail may fall on all the land of Egypt, on man and beast and every plant of the field throughout the land of Egypt.”
So Moses stretched out his staff toward heaven, and the LORD sent thunder and hail, and lightning struck the earth. So the LORD rained down hail upon the land of Egypt.
The hail fell and the lightning continued flashing through it. The hail was so severe that nothing like it had ever been seen in all the land of Egypt from the time it became a nation.
Throughout the land of Egypt, the hail struck down everything in the field, both man and beast; it beat down every plant of the field and stripped every tree.
The only place where it did not hail was in the land of Goshen, where the Israelites lived.
Then Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron. “This time I have sinned,” he said. “The LORD is righteous, and I and my people are wicked.
Pray to the LORD, for there has been enough of God's thunder and hail. I will let you go; you do not need to stay any longer.”
Moses said to him, “When I have left the city, I will spread out my hands to the LORD. The thunder will cease, and there will be no more hail, so that you may know that the earth is the LORD's.
But as for you and your officials, I know that you still do not fear the LORD our God.”
Now the flax and barley were destroyed, since the barley was ripe and the flax was in bloom;
but the wheat and spelt were not destroyed, because they are late crops.
Then Moses departed from Pharaoh, went out of the city, and spread out his hands to the LORD. The thunder and hail ceased, and the rain no longer poured down on the land.
When Pharaoh saw that the rain and hail and thunder had ceased, he sinned again and hardened his heart, he and his officials.
So Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he would not let the Israelites go, just as the LORD had said through Moses.

shem.gloss: Name, standing for a person's whole reputation and revealed character. God's stated purpose in this plague, that His name be proclaimed in all the earth, becomes the very verse Paul quotes in Romans 9:17 to ground his teaching on God's sovereign freedom in mercy and judgment.

barad.gloss: Hail. Paired here with fire flashing within it, an unnatural, terrifying combination the text stresses had never before been seen in Egypt, a direct judgment on Nut, the sky goddess, and Seth, god of storms.

basic.context: This is the seventh plague, and God pauses here to explain His purpose directly to Pharaoh: He could have destroyed Egypt already, but He is raising Pharaoh up to display His power and make His name known worldwide. Then the worst hailstorm in Egypt's history falls, mixed with fire, destroying crops and killing anyone left unsheltered outdoors. Pharaoh confesses his sin more fully than ever before, but the moment the storm stops, he hardens his heart again.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history, and it contains one of the Bible's most direct statements of God's purpose in judgment, later quoted by Paul in Romans 9 to explain God's sovereign freedom. The passage also shows one of Pharaoh's fullest, most explicit confessions of sin, and still it evaporates the moment relief comes, teaching that words of confession are not the same as a changed heart.
basic.originalLanguages: God says His shem, name, will be proclaimed in all the earth through this contest, the exact verse Paul quotes in Romans 9:17. The barad, hail, mixed with fire, was unlike anything Egypt had ever seen, a direct assault on Egypt's sky and storm deities.
basic.typology: Hail mixed with fire recurs as an image of divine judgment throughout Scripture, appearing again in Revelation 8:7 and 16:21 as end-times plagues, tying this ancient judgment on Egypt to the final judgment on a world that, like Pharaoh, refuses to repent even under devastating pressure.
basic.christ: God's stated purpose, that His name might be proclaimed in all the earth, foreshadows the greater proclamation of God's name and glory accomplished through Christ, whose name is above every name so that at the name of Jesus every knee will bow, Philippians 2:9-10, the ultimate fulfillment of God making Himself known to all the earth.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over raising up a rebellious king for His own purposes, as Paul explains directly from this passage in Romans 9. He is also merciful in the midst of judgment, giving explicit warning so that those who fear His word can shelter their households, distinguishing not only Israel from Egypt but even the fearing from the unfearing within Egypt itself.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 9:17 quotes Exodus 9:16 directly to teach God's sovereign purpose in Pharaoh's hardening. Psalm 78:47-48 and Psalm 105:32-33 both recall this plague of hail and fire. Revelation 16:21 pictures a similar plague of great hail as final judgment.
basic.oneStory: This is the plague where God explains His own purpose most plainly, that His name would be known throughout the earth, and Paul later draws directly on this exact text to teach the whole church about God's sovereign freedom. It anticipates the final judgment of Revelation and the ultimate proclamation of God's name in Christ.

context.literary: The seventh and most theologically explicit plague narrative, opening with an unusually long divine speech explaining God's purpose before any plague falls, and closing with Pharaoh's fullest confession of sin in the entire cycle, followed immediately by his most explicit renewed hardening.
context.historical: Hail is a genuine but rare meteorological event in Egypt, and a storm this severe, combined with fire and thunder, would have been read as an assault on Nut, the Egyptian sky goddess, and possibly Seth, god of storms and chaos, striking at the heart of Egyptian cosmology.
context.canonical: Exodus 9:16 becomes one of the most theologically significant verses in the entire plague narrative, directly quoted by Paul in Romans 9:17 as the foundation for his teaching on God's sovereign freedom to show mercy and to harden.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To explain God's overarching purpose in the plagues, that His power and name would be displayed and proclaimed throughout the earth, and to show that even a seemingly genuine confession of sin does not guarantee lasting repentance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes God's specific redemptive purpose for Pharaoh at a unique moment in salvation history; it is not a claim that God raises up every ruler for an identical purpose. The enduring principle, affirmed directly by Paul in Romans 9, is that God is sovereignly free in showing mercy and in hardening, and He remains just in both.

debates.question: What does Paul mean in Romans 9:17-18 by quoting this passage to say God hardens whomever He wills?

views.label: Unconditional, sovereign hardening
views.case: Paul's argument in Romans 9 stresses that God's freedom in mercy and hardening parallels His unconditional choice of Jacob over Esau before either had done anything, suggesting Pharaoh's hardening was likewise not conditioned on any prior human choice.

views.label: Hardening as a judicial response to Pharaoh's own prior rebellion
views.case: Since the plague narrative already records Pharaoh hardening his own heart multiple times before this point, chapters 8 and 9, some read God's hardening as a righteous confirming and intensifying of a direction Pharaoh had freely and repeatedly already chosen.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm Pharaoh's genuine guilt and God's genuine sovereignty; the debate concerns the precise relationship between the two, a tension the text and Paul both state plainly without fully resolving philosophically.

study.originalLanguages: God declares His shem, name, will be proclaimed in all the earth, the very phrase Paul quotes in Romans 9:17 to found his teaching on divine sovereignty. The barad, hail, mixed with fire, is described as unprecedented in Egypt's history, a direct confrontation with Egypt's sky and storm deities.

typology.type: Hail mixed with fire as divine judgment
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in the end-times plagues of Revelation 8:7 and 16:21, tying this ancient judgment to the final judgment on a world that likewise refuses to repent.

typology.type: God's name proclaimed in all the earth
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the exaltation of Christ, whose name is above every name, so that every knee will bow and every tongue confess Him as Lord, Philippians 2:9-11.

study.christ: Paul quotes Exodus 9:16 directly in Romans 9:17 to teach that God raises up even rebellious rulers to display His power and make His name known, grounding his broader argument for God's sovereign freedom in showing mercy and hardening. That same purpose, God's name proclaimed in all the earth, reaches its fullest expression in Christ, exalted to the highest place, so that at His name every knee will bow, Philippians 2:9-11, a proclamation infinitely greater than what even the plagues on Egypt accomplished.
study.god: God is sovereign enough to raise up and use even a rebellious king for His own declared purposes, as Paul explains directly from this text in Romans 9. Yet God is also merciful within judgment, giving explicit warning so that anyone who fears His word, Egyptian or Israelite, can find shelter, showing that His justice and His mercy operate together even in the midst of devastating judgment.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Exodus 9:16 directly to ground his teaching that God has mercy on whomever He wills and hardens whomever He wills.
Recalls God destroying Egypt's vines with hail and their sycamores with frost, and their livestock with lightning.
Pictures a plague of enormous hailstones as end-times judgment, echoing this ancient plague.
God's name being proclaimed in all the earth reaches its fullest expression in the exaltation of Christ.

study.oneStory: This is the plague where God states His purpose most plainly, that His power and name would be proclaimed throughout the earth, a verse Paul later builds directly upon in Romans 9. Pharaoh's confession, sincere as it may sound, evaporates the moment relief comes, showing that words of sorrow are not the same as a truly changed heart, a distinction resolved only by the deeper work of grace fulfilled in Christ.

sermon.title: The Hardening of Pharaoh and the Hope of the World
sermon.why: Though preached within Piper's series through Romans, this message works directly and at length through Exodus 9:16, Exodus 7:13, 7:22, 8:15, 8:19, and 9:12, tracing the hardening of Pharaoh's heart across exactly this section of the plague narrative and showing how Paul in Romans 9:17-18 grounds his teaching on God's sovereign freedom in this precise passage.

quiz.q: According to God's own words in verse 16, why did He raise Pharaoh up rather than simply destroying Egypt earlier?

options.text: Because Pharaoh was more righteous than the rest of Egypt.
options.note: The passage gives no indication of Pharaoh's righteousness; his stated purpose is entirely about God's display of power and name.

options.text: Because God had no power over Egypt until this point.
options.note: Verse 15 says God could have already wiped Egypt off the earth, showing He always had full power over the nation.

options.text: To display His power and to make His name proclaimed in all the earth.
options.note: Verse 16 states this purpose directly, a verse later quoted by Paul in Romans 9:17.

options.text: No reason is given for Pharaoh being raised up.
options.note: A specific reason is given plainly and directly in verse 16.

quiz.q: What warning did God give before sending the hail, and who heeded it?

options.text: He gave no warning before the hail fell.
options.note: A specific and detailed warning is given in verses 18 to 19.

options.text: He warned Egyptians to shelter their livestock and servants, and those who feared His word did so.
options.note: Verses 19 to 21 describe this warning and note that some among Pharaoh's own officials heeded it out of fear of the LORD.

options.text: The warning was given only after the hail had already fallen.
options.note: The warning explicitly preceded the hail, giving time to prepare.

options.text: Only the Israelites were warned, with no mention of any Egyptians responding.
options.note: The text specifically notes that some of Pharaoh's own officials responded to the warning by sheltering their people and animals.

quiz.q: How did Pharaoh respond immediately after the hailstorm, in verse 27?

options.text: He confessed, 'This time I have sinned,' and acknowledged that the LORD is righteous.
options.note: This is one of Pharaoh's most explicit confessions in the entire narrative, recorded directly in verse 27.

options.text: He denied any wrongdoing entirely.
options.note: The text records an explicit confession of sin, not a denial.

options.text: He blamed Moses and Aaron for causing the storm unfairly.
options.note: Rather than blaming Moses, Pharaoh explicitly confesses his own and his people's wickedness.

options.text: He made no statement at all.
options.note: His confession is directly and extensively quoted in verse 27.

quiz.q: What happened once the storm stopped, according to verses 34 to 35?

options.text: He asked Moses to make the storm return.
options.note: No such request is recorded; he simply hardened his heart again.

options.text: Pharaoh sinned again and hardened his heart, refusing to let the people go.
options.note: These verses record his immediate reversal despite his earlier confession.

options.text: He had no further reaction after the storm ended.
options.note: His renewed hardening is explicitly and emphatically recorded.

options.text: He kept his promise and released the Israelites.
options.note: The opposite occurred; he broke his word once relief was granted.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Exodus 9:16 in Romans 9?

options.text: He uses it only to discuss Egyptian agriculture.
options.note: Paul's use of this verse is entirely theological, concerning God's sovereignty in mercy and judgment.

options.text: He uses it to argue that Pharaoh was ultimately innocent.
options.note: Paul's argument affirms God's sovereignty in hardening, not Pharaoh's innocence; Pharaoh remains genuinely guilty.

options.text: He quotes it directly to ground his teaching that God has mercy on whomever He wills and hardens whomever He wills.
options.note: Romans 9:17-18 quotes this exact verse as the foundation for Paul's argument about God's sovereign freedom.

options.text: He never references this verse anywhere in his letters.
options.note: Paul quotes this verse directly and prominently in Romans 9:17.

questions: God stated His purpose plainly, that His power and name would be known throughout the earth. How does knowing that God's ultimate purpose in your own trials may be far bigger than your immediate relief change how you pray through hardship?

Some of Pharaoh's own officials feared the word of the LORD and sheltered their households, even while serving a hardened king. Where might you need the courage to respond rightly to God even when those around you, including leaders, do not?

Pharaoh's confession sounded genuine, 'The LORD is righteous, and I and my people are wicked,' yet it evaporated within hours. How can you examine whether your own repentance is rooted in true change of heart rather than a desire for relief from consequences?

Paul draws directly on this passage to teach about God's sovereign freedom in mercy and judgment, a truth many find hard to accept. What is your honest response when Scripture teaches something about God that unsettles you?

God's judgment fell on Egypt's crops precisely when barley and flax were vulnerable, sparing the later wheat and spelt. How does this level of precise timing in judgment help you trust God's precise timing in your own circumstances, even those you might call disasters?

translationNotes.note: The phrase I have raised you up can also be rendered I have spared you, both capturing the idea that Pharaoh's continued existence and rule served God's larger purpose rather than being merely an oversight or delay.

The precise timing of the destroyed and spared crops, barley and flax versus wheat and spelt, reflects accurate knowledge of the Egyptian agricultural calendar, where barley ripens and flax blooms earlier in the growing season than wheat and spelt.
